content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I just watched Se7en. What’s next?

**Score:** 103
**Body:** I just finished Se7en and I think it may be the greatest movie I’ve ever seen. Prime Fincher, prime Freeman, prime Pitt, prime Shore, everything. What should I watch now for a sort of double bill? I’m in the mood for just about anything, but I want it to be gripping. During Se7en I did not want to stop watching, ever. I was very disappointed when it ended. Anyone have any suggestions?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Zodiac or Memories of murder
  - Double down on Zodiac. My favourite Fincher.

I'll throw Chinatown and LA Confidential out there, too. Two great crime movies. Double Indemnity if you fancy a real old classic
2. Have you seen The Game with Michael Douglas?
3. Silence of the Lambs 

Prisoners

Copycat 

The bone collector
  - Prisoners is edge of your seat for 2 hours
4. Ei8ht
5. Prisioners, true detective (season 1), wind river, hell or high water, no country for old men
  - I really do treat True Detective s1 like a movie, watch the whole thing in one sitting.
6. If you loved Se7en, definitely check out Zodiac or Prisoners both are tense.
7. Hannibal - The series. I absolutely love this show. And I appreciate that Chef Jose Andres helped with creating the “food”. 
Give it a try. It’s on Amazon.

## 2. I need to see some movies with AWESOME car chases, please.

**Score:** 75
**Body:** After 14 months without a working car, I am celebrating with having been gifted a nice, retired police car. It needs a deep cleaning and a console between the seats, but that's about it. I took it out for a little joy ride around the neighborhood, AND IT IS FUN!!!! Ahem. Now I want to put together a little list of movies with awesome car chases to watch with my dog before bed at night. haha

What do you recommend? The more absurd, the better. Realism is not required.

Thank you!!!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Blues Brothers
  - It held the record for the most cars destroyed until the sequel.
2. RONIN. Great car chase scene, good movie.
3. Bullitt, Smokey and the Bandit, French Connection, Fast and Furious, Days of Thunder, The Italian Job, Ronin, Le Mans, Taxi.
4. You could always start with the absolute classic Bullit. It still holds up. Same with Smokey and the Bandit. 

Blues Brothers has a pretty iconic car chase.

One of my personal favorites is Terminator 2 although that features a motorcycle.

The Matrix Reloaded has an absolutely incredible car chase... maybe the best of all time.

Baby Driver has several great ones on top of being a great movie.

The opening sequence of Drive is incredible.
5. To Live And Die In L.A. (1985)
6. Mad Max, The Road Warrior, Mad Max Beyond Thunderdome, Mad Max: Fury Road; Maximum Risk; Gone in 60 Seconds (the original)
7. I liked the one in *The Bourne Identity*
8. The French Connection (1971)

To Live and Die in LA (1985)
9. Death Proof (the ending sequence is great).

## 3. Movies About hating your job ( i hate my job)

**Score:** 43
**Body:** Yeah so the title pretty much says it. I hate my job and my manager. I’ve seen Horrible bosses and Office Space and Fight club. Or Devil wears prada I’ve obviously seen that one.  

God i hate my job

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Clerks
2. May I suggest Falling Down? if you want a story about your avg joe that hits his absolute limit, this will work.
3. Waiting
4. Office Space
  - The OG
5. Clerks

9 To 5

Visioneers
6. Nine to Five is an absolute MUST if you haven’t seen. Swimming with Sharks, American Beauty (if you don’t mind the problematic subplot), Clockwatchers, Joe vs the Volcano. The Firm. Working Girl if you want something with an aspirational vibe?
7. The Incredibles
8. Shaun Of The Dead
9. Sorry to Bother You

## 4. Name movies that made you hold your bladder while watching them.

**Score:** 28
**Body:** We all have movies that are so interesting that made you stick to your seat throught the film hold your bladder. 
For me it's 
- Green room
- 10 cloverfield Lane

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. nearly all of them, i hate getting up once the movie starts.
2. Anything with bagpipe music makes me want to hold a bladder.
3. Return of the King, I was a kid and downed the largest drink at the theater and my bladder was working overtime,  I thought I was minutes away from relief, queue the movies 5 endings… I thought I was going to die
4. I always try to go on an empty bladder to avoid this. Most recent one was Alien Romulus, i was struggling but made it to the end and RAN to the oublic restroom.
5. The first Lord of the Rings.
6. Avengers: Endgame
  - This! I'm convinced the movie theater concession stand puts something in their beverages that makes you have to pee, in the hopes that you'll come back and see the parts you missed. When I go to the theater to see any big epic film, I generally go pee at least twice beforehand.
I know it would be easier just not to drink anything, but I just can't resist the whole "movie theater experience" with the enormous popcorn and soda.
7. Furiosa in theatres. I’d pay to see that again in iMax.
8. Raiders of the Lost Ark.  Really had to go before the movie started.  Once it started, I didn't want to miss any of it.  It was a few months before my 9th birthday.
9. Apocalypto

## 5. I need melancholic movies that feel like a warm hug

**Score:** 26
**Body:** Hi, i am looking for depressive/melancholic movies with a touch of nostalgia that feel like a warm hug. The ones that won't make u feel worse but understood in a way. In such cases i always go back to "Lost in Translation" but i feel i need something new. Can u give me any suggestions? They don't have to be similar to Lost in Translation.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Little Miss Sunshine

Captain Fantastic
  - Little Miss Sunshine!!! Little Miss Sunshine!!!
2. Life of Chuck
3. The Holdovers.
4. Perks of Being a Wallflower.
5. Amélie(2001) it's so beautiful. But it is in French with subtitles. My favorite comfort movie.
6. It's a long time since I saw it but I think "On Golden Pond" might fit the description.
7. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind 

A Walk to Remember 

Steel Magnolias 

The Green Mile
8. Under the Tuscan sun 


A good year


As good as it gets
9. Girl, Interrupted

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which fictional character did you completely change your opinion for at the end of the movie?

**Score:** 1072
**Body:** Derek from American History X.


Derek is frightening throughout the first half of the movie (if the above photo isn’t implication enough as to why). Influenced by the killing of his firefighter father by black drug dealers, as well as a far-right book author and hate columnist who becomes his mentor, Derek spends much of the first half of the movie shamelessly hating on minorities, harassing illegal immigrants, and mocking Crips on the local basketball courts. His creepy and obsessive Nazi girlfriend, Stacey, and his morbidly obese fellow skinhead Seth, are just about the only friends he really has anymore as his immediate family (save for his impressionable little brother) become disgusted by him after he causes a scene and chews out his mom’s new Jewish boyfriend, calling the man an ethnic slur. After shooting a black intruder who breaks into his vehicle and curb-stomping the intruder’s accomplice, Derek is sent to jail.

Derek changes in prison from a smartass racist to a frightened kid whose only solace is in a black fellow inmate who befriends him and shows him mercy. After a brutal rape by a white supremacist, Derek is traumatized and comes to understand how many errors he’s really made when it comes to racial prejudice. Violence is violence, no matter where it comes from, and Derek’s hatred was irrational and pointless. Upon getting out of prison, he realizes that he needs to do everything in his power to break his little brother away from going down the same dangerous trajectory that he did.

My opinion on Derek changed when he illustrated the human capacity for change, as well as the damage that parental messages in early childhood can cause. An end flashback in the movie reveals that a younger Derek deeply respected a beloved black English teacher and diverse literature, but his father broke into a racist tirade at the dinner table, goading Derek until he finally agreed with him. Derek grew up with hateful influences from a young age, and it took a great deal of self-reflection and accountability for him to understand that he’d been poisoned and used as a pawn by his so-called Aryan “friends”. He makes peace with his mother, his liberal sister and his toddler kid sister, and he and his brother tear down a variety of disturbing Nazi posters and Hitler propaganda from their shared bedroom, while his brother decides not to follow in Derek’s past footsteps.

The movie never excuses Derek’s past actions, but his character shows the vulnerability of youth when the adults around them try to use them for their own agenda. As for Derek’s author mentor, the author is a coward who gets young men and adolescent boys to run the skinhead gangs for him while he sits in his home and evades any legal responsibility for what he encourages of them.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of my favorite performances in the history of movies. Ed Norton fucking crushed it so hard. So many incredible scenes. The rage when he kills the gang bangers. The rage during the dinner scene. The sorrow in the hospital and at the end. The gratitude as he is leaving prison and says goodbye to his friend. Its so good.
  - His buddy in prison was awesome
    - Honestly, his buddy (the one he works with in the prison) is my favorite character in the movie.
      - It has to be his buddy that vouched for him with his black brothers to save him from the nazis right?
2. Jason Dixon in Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri.
  - Great answer. I don’t think I’ve ever had a bigger change of heart on a character in such a short time span. He obviously still has many faults but I went from despising him to sympathizing with him so much. Was good to see him and the main character together in the end.
3. Domnhall Gleeson’s character in the movie “Frank” starring him and Michael Fassbender. My opinion of him did a complete 180 from start to finish.
4. Well, Amy in Gone Girl is quite a wild ride.

Edit to add an honorable mention: “Kjærligheten for voksne” (I think it was something like “loving adults” in English speaking countries, but the actual translation is closer to “Love for adults”). It somewhat remembers Gone girl, but it is an even more interesting twist.
5. GRU in Despicable me
6. Memento (2000)

By design it gets you to change your opinion on all three main characters 🤷‍♂️

## 2. Fargo - Jerry Lundegaard, such an amazing weasel. Movie perfection. Well, heck, if you wanna play games here! I'm workin' with ya on this thing.

**Score:** 347

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You just gotta get that TruCoat
2. The banality of evil
  - Whether it’s *Boogie Nights* or *Fargo*, William H. Macy is gifted at playing banal.
    - my wife is down there with some guys ass in her cock!
3. Wade, it's jerry, it's Jeanie, somethings happening oh geez
4. What the heck you mean?
  - Im cooperatin' here
5. I’m not gonna debate you, Jerry!
6. But that was my deal Wade
7. Perfect writing-casting-performance

## 3. What’s a nearly perfect movie that’s ruined by one bad scene?

**Score:** 346
**Body:** I was re-watching A Quiet Place the other night and realised how one or two moments kind of yank me out of tension completely. 

That got me thinking, sometimes an awkward scene, bad delivery or weird plot choice can take an otherwise amazing movie down a notch. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My first time watching the Quiet Place I had no idea that there were supposed to be subtitles. I thought it was super interesting that you had to discern and pay attention to what the characters were trying to communicate. I thought it was a genius idea for effective story telling. 

Only when Krasinski and Blunt had a lengthy dialogue did I understand that something was wrong lol.
  - There’s a movie called Into the Void that’s pretty artsy, so when a character died and everything went silent we assumed it was some crazy choice by the director. After 30 minutes we realized our speakers had blown out.
  - Wife and I were watching the first episode of Mare of Easttown.  The show made some really interesting stylistic choices, mostly very close up shots.  Zoomed in on eyes.  Sometimes there were scenes where Kate Winslet’s character was talking to someone but they weren’t in the shot.  Felt really weird and I paused to check the settings.  Nothing was amiss.  That is until the end credits came on and they were on the very bottom right hand corner and cut off.

I closed and relaunched HBO.  Yep.  We were literally watching the top left 1/4 of the screen for a whole hour long show.

Missed entire characters in scenes.  It was still a good show!
  - Watched The Road with a friend. Thought it was an unbelievable slow slog of a movie characters. Walked slow and talk slow. After almost 2 hours and feeling there's still a lot of movie we paused when we realized we were watching it at a 70% play speed the whole time. Since it was such a slow movie already, it was hard to notice the difference.
2. By no means a perfect movie but the Robert DeNiro deaged but clearly an old man beating scene.

How could Scorsese have sat down and watched that and thought… yeah, that looks good.
3. Why would you make this post and not say what scenes they are you didn't like?
4. What was the "bad" scene in a quiet place??
5. why dont they live behind the waterfall!?
6. Let me flip that on its head and tell you about one good scene that’s ruined by an entire move

A Quiet Place: Day One

ETA, explanation: we get a good look at the monsters for once and just how horribly dangerous they are. After that the whole thing is an out of place courtship romance novel wrapped in box-office hopes

Edit 2: fix apostrophe gore
  - Great short film if it the movie ends right after it starts

## 4. What moment in a film caught you off guard completely?

**Score:** 273
**Body:** Film - **Boogie Nights**(1997)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Departed. When the elevator opens up
  - I just watched that for the first time last week, mind blown just like billy
2. Usual Suspects- the big reveal- still is amazing to watch
3. It’s so well known that it may have lost its shock value, but can you imagine what it was like to watch Psycho in a theater in 1960?
4. Alien, chest burster scene
5. The Sixth Sense

That's one of the few where the twist wasn't known going in and I saw it in theaters early in it's run.
6. Burn After Reading.

Brad Pitt in the closet.
7. Edward Norton—Primal Fear
8. Forrest Gump “Is he smart?  Or..?” 😭
9. Deep Blue Sea - Samuel Jackson’s dramatic monologue

## 5. Who’s the best mob boss?

**Score:** 235
**Body:** Who ran their crew with an iron fist 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Do you know what nemesis means?"
  - “A righteous infliction of retribution manifested by an appropriate agent. Personified in this case by an ‘horrible c*nt..  me.
  - Brick Top is my favorite too. The ultimate cockney accent
2. Vito Corleone (Marlon Brando) - The Godfather
3. Fat Tony.
4. Does Jabba the Hutt count? He is a gangster.
5. Sonny a Bronx tale. Until he got murdered, he ran
A stable crew that was loyal to him. No one turned rat on him and he had the loyalty of the neighborhood.

Paulie second place had a long run but lost control 
Of Henry and Jimmy and died in prison.
  - what’s more iconic, “now youse can’t leave” or Paulie slicing that garlic
6. Michael Corleone, hello?
7. Marcellus Wallace

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Inherent Vice!

**Score:** 63
**Body:** With the deserved popularity of Another Battle After Another, I’d love to turn some attention to a different PTA film.

Obviously our friend PTA is a big fan of the American author Thomas Pynchon. Weird names, strange plots, strong sense of paranoia. 

Inherent Vice is my favorite Paul Thomas Anderson movie and possibly my favorite movie of all time. 

Of the two Pynchon novels that Anderson has adapted to screen, I’d put Inherent Vice as the more interesting one.

Inherent Vice is Pynchon doing Raymond Chandler. A distorted and drugged out LA Noir. 

The way Anderson plays with the the zeitgeist of the 70’s, the characters and ultimately the climax is so much fun. 

These two movies (OBAA and IV) could exist in the same world and they probably do. 

What do you all think of Inherent Vice? 



### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s a great movie, very well-paced and accurately captures the paranoia and confusion of the novel, along with its humor. Usually I like to read the source material before seeing a film, but in this case I read the book after and was surprised how close of an adaptation it was. I love how strongly it establishes a sense of Doc’s neighborhood.

It’s a minor part of the film, but Martin Short’s coked-out dentist is incredibly funny.
  - > It’s a minor part of the film, but Martin Short’s coked-out dentist is incredibly funny.

That and Brolin going chickenhead on a popsicle out of nowhere.
2. I saw it in the theater on release and it left me cold and confused enough to never return to it. Finally this month I felt like it was time. I ended up watching it three times in one week and fell completely in love with it. It foregrounds the heart and the comedy, but the density and darkness of the Pynchonian tapestry of forces that make up America does somehow all make it into the movie and becomes more and more clear on rewatches. It’s among his best to me now.
3. One of my favorites of PTA’s works. Just a phenomenal film and mood piece, with stellar performances throughout. Joanna Newsom as the narrator is especially inspired, and I love its hazy, sprawling, and funny take on the genre
4. Inherent Vice is an incredibly funny movie to me personally. I think a lot of people struggle with the film because they want a tiddy resolution to it but they don't come anywhere close to it so its hard for them to get invested in the journey that we go on with Doc. However the craft and execution in it is just as good as any of his other work.
  - It’s one of the funniest movies ever made.
5. It's a movie I really want to love, but can't quite get there. Huge fan of Joaquin Phoenix and Pynchon, and even The Big Lebowski. But somehow this doesn't hit the right notes for me, despite some really stellar scenes.
6. I don't have a ton to add but did want to mention this is probably my favorite Brolin performance. I love the movie and it is among my favorite PTA films. Brolin is unbelievably funny.
  - Him deep throating the banana is an incredible sight gag
7. Brilliant film, I love it more and more each time I watch it. Such a heartbreaking lament for lost love and the promise of 60's counterculture. Bleak film about how the bad guys won and ruined every chance of a just and equitable world we could have had. 

Especially love the Josh Brolin character Bigfoot Bjornsen. So many layers and nuance there, vividly captured by PTA in writing and Brolin's performance. And like most PTA movies, so so so funny

## 2. Dogtooth – How did the kids not notice this?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I watched Lanthimos' Dogtooth last night, and one detail keeps bothering me. The parents tell their children that once a canine tooth falls out, they are ready to leave home. When it grows back, they can safely drive away from home. It's clearly a rule invented to control them.

But here's the thing: children naturally lose their baby teeth, including their canines, and they grow back. Is it possible that the children didn't notice this? Wouldn't they have questioned the rule, especially since it has presumably been in place since they were born?

I understand that it's all metaphorical, but it seems absurd to me that another element that could easily resolve this inconsistency wasn't exploited (for example: when your thumb falls off and grows back, you can leave the house).



I'm curious to know if anyone else has thought about this. Is there a deeper symbolic meaning that I'm missing?



### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The movie is about social control, through a family- it isn’t driven by authentic rites of passage or actual anatomy or biology- the tension is driven by the years of abusive restriction and fabricated social interactions that maintain a strict hierarchy on age and gender lines within a family cult.

So the dogtooth, like the airplane, are intentionally warped models of reality that the father asserts rather than justifies. 

Even if the kids were smart enough when they were children to make this logical leap, it’s an easy fix for the father: nope, you were wrong. Those were not your dogteeth. Those were your puppy teeth, and you have passed through your first rite, on your way to the the journey. In fact, you have noticed a secret, congratulations: your lost teeth and newly grown dogteeth *prove to you* what will happen again- when your dogteeth now fall out and regrow, you will make it through your next rite.
  - Yes exactly this.

I also think we are shown that the children do have a childlike curiosity even in their later ages, often asking what different words mean. And the mother is always there to give an answer that teaches the kids distorted ideas about how things truly operate. And it isn't until they start bringing in the security guard that the Eldest starts to realize she can even question her parents at all.
    - Exactly. They are weaponizing language to enforce control, all the time.
2. sorry if i'm misremembering - but aren't they told about the dogtooth relatively late in their lives? i didn't think it was in place since they were born. 

my guess for why the tooth was chosen is that the removal of the tooth is more evocative in comparison to any other body part. other body parts would be still be gnarly but there's something really harrowing in particular about the brick and the face that i think makes it the best choice for the film even if it isn't the most logical choice.
3. I don't remember how it's worded but they basically say when the *second* dog tooth falls out and comes back.


So it's actually a pretty great trick because they know that teeth can and do grow back and that it has something to do with maturing, but they don't know that this will never happen for them.
4. Kids don't have all teeth lol

You have 20 baby teeth that fall out, but your full adult set of teeth has 32. So those twelve never fall out.

I need more characters because the rules, lalalalalalalalala
  - Yes but.. honestly don't understand the correlation. Even though they don't have all their teeth, canines are baby teeth. Consequently, they will have fallen out and grown back during childhood.

So the requirement of “losing your baby teeth and waiting for them to grow back” was already fulfilled during childhood.

Knowing how resourceful and curious children are at that age, did no one ask any questions or throw any tantrums? Did no one try to leave the house?
5. From what I rememeber the children could leave ghe house when the wisdom teeth fall out, and use a car when they regrow. Wisdom teeth arenlt part pf the childrens theaet. So the criterea to leave can”t never met. Wisdom teeth was how it was translated in dutch subtitels.

## 3. If you had an assessment where you interrogated the concept of the 'human experience', what movie would you think best suits this?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I’m currently working on an assessment that requires me to explore and analyze the concept of the *human experience* — a broad and layered theme that encompasses identity, consciousness, morality, emotion, relationships, struggle, growth, mortality, and more. It’s a term often used in academic and literary contexts, but one that can be interpreted in countless ways depending on the lens through which it's viewed.

To deepen my understanding, I’m turning to film as a medium — because cinema has an incredible ability to reflect, question, and challenge our understanding of what it means to be human. I’m not just looking for films that *represent* the human experience, but those that truly **interrogate** it. By that, I mean films that ask questions, pose dilemmas, or unravel aspects of human life in a way that forces the viewer to reflect more deeply on existence, purpose, or identity.

Here’s the key: whatever film is suggested needs to hold **academic value**. It should offer enough thematic or philosophical weight to be critically analyzed — something that could stand up to deeper discussion, whether in terms of social commentary, existential themes, narrative structure, or symbolic imagery. I'm not necessarily looking for what’s *popular*, but what’s *thought-provoking*. Preferably one with lots of articles/discussions written on it.

Some examples that come to mind include *The Truman Show*, *Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind*, *Her*, *Ikiru*, or *Synecdoche, New York* — films that blur the line between cinematic storytelling and philosophical inquiry.

So, I’m asking: **What film do you think best interrogates the human experience, and why?** I’d really appreciate hearing your thoughts, especially if you can explain how or why it holds academic or analytical value.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 
There are some great non-fiction films that touch on this (though not sure if they fit your criteria): Minding the Gap, Hoop Dreams first come to mind.

PTAs The Master I think is a great example of the “interrogation” that you talk about: Id vs ego, the nature of, well, human nature. The Tree of Life too, A Hidden Life I think too probably does an even better job of forcing us to really think about our lives and purpose,
2. I think Melancholia covers a lot of range philosophically. It's written and directed by a known misanthrope which is interesting to confront in an academic setting.

Also, I'd encourage you to consider something Cronenberg. We tend to think about the philosophical and intellectual when we think about "the human experience" but the core shared experience of all human beings is occupying and being limited by a body. In the west we tend to write the body off, thinking of it as a meat bag or animalistic and base. But somatic therapy and other psychological research on the mind-body connection illustrates that our minds aren't separated from our body, we're whole and entire. Maybe a Cronenberg and then Sally Potter's Orlando.
3. *The Tree of Life*

To relate it to your question, it captures the phenomenology of childhood in a way I’ve never seen a film come close to, and as a result is able to ask questions about the unfolding of consciousness over time, which it also interrogates on a more metaphysical level in its creation sequence. 

That development of consciousness can also be related to its epigraph, God’s challenge to Job in Chapter 38, Verse 4:
“Where were you when I laid the earth’s foundation?
    Tell me, if you understand.
 Who marked off its dimensions? Surely you know!
    Who stretched a measuring line across it?
 On what were its footings set,
    or who laid its cornerstone—
 while the morning stars sang together
    and all the angels shouted for joy?”

The film explores the nature of grief, specifically the grief of a mother losing her child, an experience that may have anybody questioning the nature of God and the existence of this cosmic cruelty. Is this epigraph played straight? Is Malick offering an aesthetic theodicy and seeking to answer this challenge? How could this question be fairly posed to a mortal in the first place? I love Jung’s *Answer to Job* and think about it often when pondering this film. 

Malick’s academic background almost inevitably follows in discussions on his work. He was famously a Heideggerian who studied under Stanley Cavell at Harvard. People often read Heidegger into his work for this reason. It’s very fun to ask “well why did he leave the academy? What did film offer him that academic philosophy didn’t? What could he communicate via film that he couldn’t as a philosopher?” 

Just scratching the surface here. This film is life itself to me, but I’ll be so bold to say I think it is one of the best answers to your question.
4. For me Synecdoche, New York nearly stands on its own for its interrogation of the human experience, "all the world's a stage and all the men and women merely players" taken to its absurd conclusion.


It really questions why we do anything at all, work, love, art, etc.
5. John Cassavetes' LOVE STREAMS. Why do we: love, live, search, battle? Why do we insist, even through all the hardship? What's the strenght? Why do some people leave and others stay? Why? Eternal answer, and Cassavetes is so subtle. It's the human experience in two hours. Utterly heartbreaking and beautiful.

"Well, life is a serius of suicides, divorces, promises broken, children smashed, whatever..."
  - My favorite Cassavetes film, and very underrated.
6. I think either Blade Runner movie would lend itself well to this lens. A major conceit of that franchise is the idea that humanity is defined by experience. Really any sci fi that explores humanity through the lens of artificial life should have plenty to sink your teeth into.

## 4. Reflections on rewatching Atonement

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Enough couldn't be said about Joe Wright's masterpiece. The first half of the film holds you spellbound. It is like he's spinning you in the air, capturing the cusp of youth and passion in a single dazzling motion. The contrast between the severity of childhood and the verdant green of summer, of first love, plays so well with the parallel perspectives we see. 

Then, in the second half, he plunges us into misery. The sweeping takes at Dunkirk and the dizzying activity at the hospital where Briony works with the flux of soldiers are both breathtaking. When I was younger, I only appreciated the more obvious scenes. The fountain scene, the one with the green dress, the spectacular c*nt sequence that begins with Robbie in the bath and ends with the flash of green as Cecilia is getting dressed, the clickety clack of the evergoing typewriter, and the grand deception of the final act. 

But now, I see the finer details a bit more clearly, the desolation of all three of them, the theme of water, the airplane Robbie sees in the bath and hums at and sees again reflected in the water before he ends up at Dunkirk, the hopelessness and guilt at having her waiting, all based on one summer evening.

It's a heartbreaking film, one that you appreciate more and more as you grow older and begin to understand what they lost. 

Watching this movie, you are, on the surface, tied to your seat. But it is a visceral, absorbing experience where you are now in the air and now in the abyss, left completely to the whims of a man who is a master of his craft. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I really despise the ending.  Spoiler for those that haven’t seen it.

The fact that Briony becomes a successful writer writing about the people’ lives see ruined, as her last book, that led to their deaths is such a cop out.  She does not have the right to give the people’s lives she ruined a happy ending because of her guilt.  I found it to be incredibly selfish and utter bullshit that she is forgiving herself for her selfish acts.  It shows she never changed and just cares about herself.  I wanted to just jump through the screen and bunch her in The face.
  - Atonement is something Bryony strives toward, not necessarily something she achieves. The film (and the novel) imply that her actions have weighed so heavily on her as to alter her life. She does not necessarily believe that she has atoned, but her life has become entirely dedicated to the idea of atonement. So, you’re well within your rights to believe that she has failed (I would agree with you) but the film doesn’t necessarily make a decision either way; it only shows how much she has been searching for a way to make up for her mistake.
    - I totally agree with what you are saying.  I just disagree with entire approach to the ending.  Why does she get to be a successful writer?  Why approach that narrative in the book and movie, I guess, took in making her successful.  Maybe it says more about the author than anything.
      - It’s a fair point, but I think that her success is an indication of the intensity of her regret. I may be misremembering, but I think that (perhaps in one of the later interviews scenes?) she tells the interviewer that she tried to give Robbie and her sister the resolution that she could not achieve in real life. The interview becomes a kind of confession, an admission that her decades spent trying to write a satisfying ending just don’t work. Even the book she did publish is not the ending she wanted, because she has to go on tv and admit that it’s all fiction. Her success has meant nothing to her, because she always comes back to this story, this mistake, and she still can’t get it right, even as her body begins to fail her. There is nothing she can really do to atone, but she is running out of time. 

I’ve always thought about it a bit like the Henry Hill ending in Goodfellas, as the unreliable narrator speaks directly to the audience to confess to her crimes. They throw themselves on the mercy of the audiences’ judgement but it doesn’t really matter, because no judgement will be more damning than the life that they are forced to live as a consequence of their actions. I don’t think the ending rewards Bryony at all; I think she’d agree with you that she doesn’t deserve any of her success and her entire life has been a desperate, failing attempt to make up for that. It’s a life in which any success she has achieved as a writer is undermined by her immense guilt and the constant reminder of the limitations of her power to change anything or to achieve true atonement. That she gets a life at all is a burden to her compared to the lives she took from others.
        - I see what you mean but I think it is just a cop out that she has lived her whole life.  She waited until she was old and successful before she allowed the world to judge her.  She didn't do it 10-15 years earlier where the could have to deal with the judgement from her fans, world, etc.  At this point if she doesn't want to hear the judgement of others she can go and be a recluse it's her last book and live her life knowing she at least got it off her chest.  It is still a selfish way out no matter what.
      - I think it’s childish and vaguely repugnant to accuse the author of some vague immorality because you got angry about a challenging story.

Why does she get to be a successful writer? Because moral worth has nothing to do with fame and fortune.

Good things do not happen to good people, and believing they do does incalculable damage to our lives.
  - You're saying you despised the film's ending? Or the character in the film?
    - Both
  - I hate injustice too, but I have zero problems with a film for showing it.

And I hate how common narrative causality is, that you get angry with the movie when bad things don’t happen to a bad person. Or when people are displayed as multifaceted - especially ones who have done unforgivable wrongs.

This has become such an issue that just showing something in art is often described as ‘promoting’ it, and artists are increasingly derided for anything that isn’t a morality play.

The story challenged you and challenged your worldview. That’s a good thing! Respect it for that!

## 5. A Vanished film

**Score:** 2
**Body:** Hi guys, i was reading the memoir of a french/Canadian actress (Alexandra Stewart) and she mentioned a film that she has acted and it is called Le Soleil en face (1980), and believe it or not this film is not at ANY streaming services or any websites including internet archive. There are only photos of it but nothing further. It is directed by Pierre Kast who the actress was very close with, and he sounds like a legit intellectual person. 

I am curious of the film so i emailed BFI (British Film Institute) by the advice of chatgpt, did anyone ever emailed BFI to view a film? It claims that it is free to view films in person at BFI (London), is it true? 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. >and believe it or not this film is not at ANY streaming services or any websites including internet archive

There's quite a number of non-Hollywood films that are not available for streaming or even physical media. If even to this day, certain films by Jean Luc Godard, Claude Chabrol or Jean Renoir are not available, then what are the odds for the works of lesser known directors? Off the top of my head, some movies by French/Canadian director Gilles Carle have not been available for decades in any consumer format.
  - Oh i am not very familiar with this underrated director concept, i have seen louis malle films on internet archive so i thought it was the case for a lot directors. 

May i ask where do you watch films of such directors?
2. I agree with you that this appears to have never been released on home media in any form. Even on eBay there are several copies of the poster and lobby cards up for auction, but no VHS or DVD.
  - yeah thank u for ur reply
3. There are movie full length feature films lost than currently exist, I think. Or they are pretty neck and neck. Most reasons is that it was insanely expensive to store film correctly so most of the crappier or less profitable ones were just thrown out. 

People find old copies of lost movies in their grandpa's cellar or barn all the time. Even now things are still being found, 100 years or more after movies first really started gaining popularity.
